_comment: Sample fixture for testing the verify-brief.ts harness against a synthetic 'Yerlan' brief. Hand-crafted to exercise all 6 spec tests + their happy paths. Not a real brief — generated specifically to demonstrate what a passing brief looks like.

intake.topActivity: Math Olympiad regional bronze, debate captain
intake.background: introverted policy nerd who reads more than I should, raised in Astana by two engineers
intake.extracurriculars: debate captain since 11th grade, regional Math Olympiad bronze, ran a study group for AP Calc kids

dbContext: University of Toronto (Canada) — large research university with strong CS and policy programs. McGill (Canada) — bilingual research university, dual-degree friendly. University of British Columbia (Canada) — West-Coast tech-with-humanities crossover. University of Edinburgh (United Kingdom) — Scotland's flagship, STEM-with-policy depth. University of Glasgow (United Kingdom) — strong economics + computer science. NUS Singapore — English-medium, scholarship-heavy, visa-realistic for CIS students.

archetype.reason: intake mentions both Math Olympiad (STEM) and debate captain (humanities/policy) — strong cross-domain signal

plan.identityClaim: a policy-leaning STEM kid who hasn't told anyone yet
plan.pileContrast: Most kids in your year go in the IT-track pile or the engineering kids being pushed into Western banking since 9th grade.
plan.essaySeedType: a moment where math showed up inside a debate round

primaryGap.reason: Yerlan put CS on the intake but picked not_at_all on certainty. The indecision is the load-bearing thing to surface warmly.

plan.mondayMoveArtifact: a Google Doc titled 'math in debate' listing three specific moments

whereYouStand.headline: You're a policy-leaning STEM kid who hasn't told anyone yet.
whereYouStand.lead: Yerlan, your profile is more interesting than you're treating it.
whereYouStand.body: Most kids in your year go in one pile. The IT-track pile, the engineering kids being pushed into Western banking since 9th grade, the finance kids. They look the same on paper because the path was picked for them. Your file shows AP Calc and a Math Olympiad bronze AND a debate captain title — two things that don't normally appear together in your school's outbound applicants. That puts you in a smaller pile most admissions readers don't get to see often.
whereYouStand.pullquote: The time will come to lead with that overlap.

whereYouCanLand.headline: Three kinds of places that fit how you actually move.
whereYouCanLand.lead: Pulled from your countries, with your file in mind.

schools.name: University of Toronto
schools.lore: Where the kid running policy resolutions on weekends finds three other people doing the same in the same dorm. Big enough to disappear into when you need to, dense enough that the overlap shows up at lunch.

schools.name: McGill
schools.lore: Policy-school energy without giving up the math. The dual-major kids here aren't unusual — they're the ones who pick the program.

schools.name: University of Edinburgh
schools.lore: Where the STEM-with-policy combination isn't unusual, it's the standard. Not Oxbridge. The smarter middle.

schools.name: NUS Singapore
schools.lore: English-medium, scholarship-heavy, six-hour flight from Astana. The place that gets your visa stamped the first time you ask.

whatToWrite.kicker: 03 · The essay only you can write
whatToWrite.headline: The essay you should write doesn't exist yet — but the seed for it does.
whatToWrite.lead: One specific kind of moment to find. Then it starts itself.

essaySeed.title: The math-in-debate moment
essaySeed.body: Sometime in the last two years, something from a math class showed up inside a debate round Yerlan was running. Maybe a stat that turned the room. Probably a calculation done mid-rebuttal that made the other side stop. Maybe a piece of game theory that explained why a strategy worked. Find that exact moment.

whatsBlockingYou.headline: You don't know what you want to study yet.
whatsBlockingYou.lead: Not really — and that's information, not a flaw.

entries.title: You don't know what you want to study yet
entries.whyItMatters: Yerlan, you put 'CS' on the intake form because that's what people expect when you're good at math. That's not the same as wanting it. You've been holding the question quietly — what does it mean if you don't know yet?
entries.actionThisMonth: Stop hiding the question. The essay seed next to this one names where to start writing — let the unanswered question be part of the application.
entries.next60Days: As Yerlan talks to more schools, the question sharpens; the answer follows the conversations, not the form fields.

whatToDoThisMonth.headline: Open a Google Doc titled 'math in debate' this week.
whatToDoThisMonth.lead: One move that opens the rest.

mondayMove.headline: Open a Google Doc titled 'math in debate' this week.
mondayMove.body: Inside, list three specific times something from a math class showed up in a debate round Yerlan ran. Don't polish. Don't make them sound profound. Just three lines, one per moment. Stop when you have three.
mondayMove.closer: Once those three lines exist, the essay has somewhere to start.
